data.title: Palantir Q4 earnings preview: AI partnerships and defense growth in focus
data.description:
Palantir (PLTR) is set to report fourth-quarter earnings on Monday, and investors will watch out for the company’s expanding artificial intelligence ((AI)) partnerships as well as defense contracts growth amid global political uncertainty.

Wall Street expects the company to post earnings per share of $0.23, implying a 64.3% year-over-year increase, while revenue is expected to rise 61.9% to $1.34B for the quarter.

Palantir’s CFO David Glazer, during the Q3 earnings call, said that the firm set Q4 2025 revenue guidance at $1.329B and raised full-year 2025 revenue guidance midpoint to $4.398B, a 53% year-over-year increase and $252M above last quarter's guidance. 

Analysts are bullish about Palantir on the back of stronger demand from governments amid geopolitical tensions as well as its growth in commercial markets.

Citi Research upgraded PLTR to Buy/High Risk from Neutral/High Risk, increasing the price target to $235 from $210, implying nearly 55% upside from Thursday’s close of $151.86.

“2026 themes around Enterprise AI+ Agentic expansion and the renewed urgency around defense globally are acutely aligned to PLTR’s strength around data ontology and forward-deployed engineering,” Citi analyst Tyler Radke highlighted, adding that, “We expect significant upside revisions to FY26 estimates driven by the strength of US Commercial, where its remaining deal value (RDV) has grown at triple digits every quarter in FY25.”

In terms of commercial expansion, the firm, during its Q3 earnings call, said that customers are now approaching Palantir for enterprise-wide deployments rather than single-use cases.

Phillip Capital initiated a Buy rating with a price target of $208, implying nearly 37% upside from Thursday’s close of $151.86.

The brokerage firm, in its research note, stated that the company’s revenue mix is shifting, with commercial revenue projected to grow 51%, outpacing government revenue at 43%, driven by increased AI adoption in enterprises and expansion beyond defense into broader industry use cases.

Seeking Alpha analysts also consider it a Buy.

Palantir’s shares have gained 90% over the past one year, outperforming the broader S&P 500, which rose over 15% during the same period.

However, Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system and Wall Street analysts are cautious, rating PLTR a Hold.

Over the last 2 years, PLTR has beaten EPS estimates 100% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 100% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS and revenue estimates have seen 21 and 20 upward revisions, respectively, with no downward revisions

data.title: Protalix says EU panel recommended a new dosing regimen for Fabry disease therapy
data.description:
Protalix BioTherapeutics (PLX) said on Friday that an expert panel of the European Medicines Agency ((EMA)), endorsed a new dosing regimen for Elfabrio, an enzyme replacement therapy developed by the biotech with its Italian partner Chiesi for a rare disorder called Fabry disease.

Shares of the New Jersey-based biotech added ~14% in reaction. 

Issuing a positive opinion, the EMA’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use has endorsed the approval of a 2 mg/kg every-4-weeks dosing regimen of Elfabrio, which could ease the treatment burden on patients receiving the therapy.

The new regimen is targeted at adults with Fabry disease who are stable with an enzyme replacement therapy.

Currently, Fabry disease patients need to visit infusion centers once every two weeks to receive Elfabrio. The “CHMP positive opinion supporting an every-four-week dosing regimen brings us one step forward to further reducing treatment burden in this condition,” said Giacomo Chiesi, Executive Vice President of Chiesi Global Rare Diseases.

The CHMP recommendation will next be reviewed by the European Commission ((EC)), and a final decision on authorization, expected by March 2026, could generate a regulatory milestone payment of $25M to Protalix (PLX).

data.title: ExxonMobil targets 65% production from advantaged assets by 2030 as technology transforms portfolio
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Darren Woods highlighted that "2025 was a year of exceptional execution and technology-driven differentiation," emphasizing that ExxonMobil delivered on all 10 key 2025 projects and has "built a higher return, lower cost technology-led company." Woods stated, "We've already achieved our 2030 emission reduction plans for GHG emissions and flaring intensity," and, as of 2025, "reduced our corporate GHG intensity by more than 20%, reduced upstream GHG intensity by more than 40% and reduced corporate flaring intensity by more than 60%."
  • Woods noted upstream production averaged 4.7 million oil-equivalent barrels per day, with unit earnings more than double those in 2019 on a constant price basis. He affirmed, "We successfully delivered all 10 key 2025 projects further strengthening our portfolio and positioning us for long-term profitable growth."
  • The CEO stated, "Production from advantaged assets, including the Permian, Guyana and LNG continues to grow," and projected that these assets "will make up roughly 65% of total production by 2030."
  • Woods shared that in the Permian, "We delivered a new production record in the fourth quarter, 1.8 million oil-equivalent barrels per day, driving the highest annual company production in over 40 years." Technology deployment is central, with lightweight proppant in 25% of wells in 2025 and an expectation to reach 50% by the end of 2026.
  • The company completed $20 billion in share repurchases during the year, retiring shares equivalent to one third of those issued during the Pioneer transaction.
  • Woods highlighted technological advances, including the scaling of Proxxima systems and the exceptional performance of the advanced battery anode graphite program.
  • CFO Kathryn Mikells stated, "Our captured savings is greater than all other IOC savings combined over the same period," adding that cost reductions are driving "industry-leading earnings and cash flow even in periods of lower commodity prices."

Outlook

  • Woods stated, "Our transformed company will continue to build on this success in 2026 with higher structural earnings power, stronger mix, lower breakevens and a portfolio designed to perform across commodity cycles."
  • The company expects Permian production to show "significant improvement going into 2026 versus 2025 on an annual basis," with an annual increase of about 200,000 oil-equivalent barrels per day year-over-year, according to Mikells.
  • Woods expects to "exceed 2.5 million oil-equivalent barrels a day beyond 2030" in the Permian.
  • The company projects that "advantaged assets" will constitute approximately 65% of total production by 2030.

Financial Results

  • Woods reported, "Over the past 5 years, our annualized shareholder return of 29% has led the industry, supported by $150 billion of distributions to shareholders during that period."
  • The company continues to deliver "industry-leading earnings power, stronger cash flow potential and more profitable barrels and products."
  • ExxonMobil achieved "the highest annual company production in over 40 years at 4.7 million oil-equivalent barrels per day."
  • Structural cost savings reached $15 billion through 2025, with Mikells stating, "That's more than any of our competitors combined."
  • The company executed $20 billion in share repurchases, significantly reducing the dilutive impacts of the Pioneer acquisition.

Q&A

  • Devin McDermott, Morgan Stanley, asked about ExxonMobil's strategy in Guyana given the force majeure and upcoming block expiry. Woods responded, "We still think there's opportunity in that space to explore in the block that we can currently access," pointing to the International Court of Justice ruling as a critical milestone for the disputed area.
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs, inquired about Permian production cadence and the impact of lightweight proppant. Woods cautioned against extrapolating quarterly results and emphasized long-term improvements, saying, "We're still very focused on bringing these technologies to bear in our Cube design focused on maximum recovery and doing it at a lower cost."
  • Doug Leggate, Wolfe Research, asked about potential upside from reentering markets like Libya, Iraq, and Venezuela. Woods explained, "We're getting an opportunity to look at a number of these locations and start working with them...I do think there will be some upside out there." On Venezuela, Woods said, "Given the current fiscal structures in place: legal, that you couldn't invest, but that there was opportunities to address that."
  • Bob Brackett, Bernstein, discussed asset portfolio refreshment. Woods stated, "The advantaged assets are -- the advantage derives from what we bring to the development of those assets... that's not going to change for us."
  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan, focused on LNG projects. Woods said, "My expectation is with Mozambique, we'll see something here as we move through this year, probably on the back half of the year with respect to an FID if things go, kind of, to plan."
  • Wei Jiang, Barclays, inquired about the data system transformation. Woods detailed, "That model and that discipline is going to underpin this new ERP system, one data construct for the entire corporation, one data set, one set of nomenclatures." Mikells added, "We're going to have 97% fewer profit centers. 70% fewer cost centers."
  • Sam Margolin, Wells Fargo, pursued the battery business. Woods described, "Our technology organization, developed a molecule that has properties that really lend themselves to battery applications that result in these types of performance, step changes in performance with batteries."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive recognition of ExxonMobil’s technology execution, but several questions focused on the durability of growth in Guyana and the Permian, as well as the scalability of technological advantages and potential new market entries. Questions regarding block expiries, force majeure, and asset divestitures indicated underlying questions about long-term growth and risk.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident and forward-looking, with Woods emphasizing transformation, industry leadership, and long-term strategy. In the Q&A, Woods maintained a measured optimism, directly addressing risks and uncertainties but reiterating confidence: "We're pretty optimistic."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident, but the Q&A featured more probing on future upside from portfolio refreshment and asset expansion, reflecting heightened analyst attention to future growth drivers and risk factors.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter highlighted the completion of all 10 key 2025 projects and the achievement of previously stated emissions targets. In contrast to Q3, discussion shifted more to future upside from possible reentry into markets like Venezuela, Libya, and Iraq and the scaling of technology across the portfolio.
  • Management continued to focus on technology and cost leadership, but this quarter included more emphasis on enterprise-wide digital transformation and its projected operational impact.
  • Analysts in both quarters pressed for details on production growth and technology scalability, though current questions more frequently addressed the longevity of advantaged assets and opportunities for further expansion.
  • Key metrics such as Permian production and structural cost savings were reiterated as major strengths, but this quarter added more narrative around divestitures and portfolio high-grading.

Risks and Concerns

  • Woods acknowledged geopolitical risks in Guyana, referencing the ongoing border dispute with Venezuela and its implications for the Stabroek block.
  • Management identified the challenge of bringing new markets online, particularly where fiscal regimes and legal infrastructure are not yet conducive to investment.
  • The company cited continued supply-side margin pressure in Chemicals despite strong demand, with Woods noting, "there continues to be a lot of capacity that comes on that expresses the margin."
  • Analysts raised concerns about underlying base decline rates in the upstream and the need for ongoing technological improvement to offset depletion, though management did not provide a specific decline rate figure.

Final Takeaway

ExxonMobil's fourth quarter 2025 call underscored the strength of its technology-led transformation, with record production in the Permian, expansion of advantaged assets, and significant progress in digital and operational efficiency. Management’s disciplined portfolio strategy, focus on scalable technology deployment, and ongoing cost leadership position the company for continued growth and shareholder value creation, even as it navigates geopolitical uncertainties and evolving market dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Eastman Chemical outlines $225M–$250M cost reduction plan as earnings recovery builds through 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eastman Chemical Company (EMN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Mark Costa, Chairman & CEO, emphasized stabilization efforts in the Fibers segment, referencing the impact of tariffs and consumer pressure on textiles and the overall stream slowdown in cellulosics. Costa explained, "We feel good that we've stabilized the volume situation this year relative to last year in our contracts with our customers. It did include a bit of a modest price decline to make that happen." He highlighted ongoing cost reduction actions, targeting $125 million to $150 million in cuts atop $100 million previously achieved, with a portion allocated to Fibers. The company is expanding its product mix in textiles and ramping up Aventa for food trays and cutlery. Costa noted, "We're pulling every lever we've got. This is an incredibly important source of earnings and even more important source of cash flow, and we take this business very seriously."
  • Addressing Chemical Intermediates (CI), Costa described the ETP project converting ethylene to propylene, projecting "somewhere to $50 million to $100 million improvement in earnings and the payback on that is less than 2 years from a capital point of view." He acknowledged ongoing earnings volatility due to global market pressures, especially from Chinese exports, while stating, "There are a lot of actions we're taking to improve this business long term."
  • William McLain, CFO & Executive VP, commented on the circular solution for packaging, stating, "As we think about 2025 to 2026, volume growth is a key aspect of that, and we've highlighted that with the contracts that we have across a spectrum of key brands that we're growing with in the packaging space and that being the substantial driver."

Outlook

  • The company expects a meaningful improvement in earnings for 2026, with Costa confirming, "when you think about the sort of upper end of what you're talking about around $6 a share, that's very much in the range of what we're thinking is possible." He emphasized uncertainty due to macroeconomic conditions but pointed to substantial cost reductions ($225 million to $250 million over two years) and volume growth via innovation as key levers. The guidance reflects stable volumes in Fibers and growth in Advanced Materials (AM), with the circular economy platform and rPET contracts, including Pepsi, driving revenue increases.

Financial Results

  • Costa noted that Q1 is expected to start "a little bit light" in Fibers but show volume recovery in AM and CI as destocking abates. The company experienced higher energy costs and some modest price declines in CI and Fibers. McLain indicated, "we had to do some of our own destocking" and that "as these volumes come back, we start getting a meaningful tailwind on utilization in this segment." While specific quarterly EPS or revenue figures were not disclosed, management highlighted cost reduction momentum and improved asset utilization as contributors to earnings recovery.

Q&A

  • Joshua Spector (UBS): Asked about Fibers shutdown impact and cellulosics pricing. Costa responded that stabilization actions included cost reductions, expanding textile offerings, and ramping up Aventa. Contracted volumes are stable to last year, with price adjustments included.
  • David Begleiter (Deutsche Bank): Inquired about CI volatility management and Q1 EPS bridge. Costa detailed the ETP project’s structural improvement and projected volume recovery across segments, noting, "overall, we feel like we've got a meaningful amount of volume recovery coming our way."
  • Rachael Lee (Citi): Questioned methanolysis margins and 2026 specialty pricing. McLain explained mixed drop-through margins and volume-driven growth, while Costa acknowledged modest price declines in AM as cost advantages are shared, offset by volume gains.
  • Aleksey Yefremov (KeyBanc): Estimated EPS range for 2026. Costa stated, "the upper end... around $6 a share, that's very much in the range of what we're thinking is possible," but noted macro uncertainty.
  • John Hendricks (Morgan Stanley): Sought details on Advanced Materials tailwinds. Costa highlighted volume growth, cost reduction, utilization gains, and FX benefits, offset by energy costs and modest price declines.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, probing for clarity on volume stabilization, cost reductions, and margin drivers but frequently returned to macroeconomic uncertainty and earnings visibility. Questions on contract stability, asset utilization, and pricing reflected a pragmatic, slightly skeptical tone.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured stance, frequently citing robust cost discipline and innovation-driven volume growth. Costa’s language around control—"we feel good," "we're pulling every lever," and "we're very focused on controlling what we can control"—reinforced a tone of proactive management amid uncertainty. Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive about cost reduction impact and volume stabilization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s guidance language shifted from caution in Q3—emphasizing recovery only as macro allowed—to a more assertive tone in Q4, detailing concrete cost-reduction targets and stabilization in Fibers. The focus on the ETP project and expanded circular economy efforts in Q4 contrasted with Q3’s broader discussion of market-driven recovery. Analysts in both quarters pressed for clarity on volume recovery and cost impacts but in Q4 sought finer details on contract stability and incremental earnings levers. The confidence in achieving $225 million to $250 million in cost reductions over two years was more pronounced in this call.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued macroeconomic uncertainty, volatile global demand, and elevated energy costs as ongoing headwinds. Destocking pressure in Fibers may persist into Q1, and the company is not assuming market improvement from global rationalization or Chinese capacity dynamics in guidance. Structural challenges in CI and potential downside from regulatory bans in European crop protection were acknowledged. Management’s mitigation strategies include aggressive cost controls, asset optimization, and diversification of product offerings in Fibers and AM.

Final Takeaway

Eastman Chemical’s management signaled a multi-pronged recovery strategy for 2026, anchored by $225 million to $250 million in cost reductions, volume growth in key segments, and stabilized performance in Fibers and Chemical Intermediates. The company’s outlook includes a focus on innovation and expanded circular economy initiatives, with actions designed to deliver meaningful earnings improvement despite acknowledged economic headwinds and market uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Primis Financial outlines path to 1% ROA in 2026 with margin expansion and core deposit growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Primis Financial Corp. (FRST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Dennis Zember highlighted that the company reported earnings of $29.5 million or $1.20 per share for the quarter, noting "in the fourth quarter of '25, Matt and I are showing our run rate earnings at about $8 million, which works out to about an 80 basis point ROA on about $4 billion of average assets." Zember emphasized substantial gains from a sale leaseback and related restructuring, but pointed to a run rate that reflects “virtually no improvement from the restructure.”
  • Zember stated, "our margin increased from 2.90% in the fourth quarter of last year to 3.28% in the fourth quarter of this year," and highlighted checking account growth of over 23% for the year, leveraging the company’s proprietary app and success in growing noninterest-bearing deposits to 16.3% of total deposits by year-end.
  • The CEO described rebuilding earning assets with a $325 million increase for the year, and noted, “the thing that's really driving the bottom line improvement or the ROA improvement is operating leverage.”
  • Zember acknowledged, “we finished '25 with $993 million in digital deposits, which is down maybe less than 10% from where we were a year ago despite the fact that the rate is down 115 basis points.”
  • On mortgage, Zember reported Primis Mortgage closed $1.2 billion in loans, a 50% rise over 2024, with $143 million closed in December alone, and pre-tax quarterly earnings of $1.4 million.
  • CFO Matthew Switzer stated, “gross loans held for investment increased approximately 10% annualized from September 30 to December 31,” and “net interest income was approximately $31 million, a substantial improvement from $26 million in the year-ago period.” Switzer also noted the net interest margin reached 3.28% in Q4, with expectations for further margin expansion in 2026.

Outlook

  • Management stated their “bogey is still for the full year, a 1% ROA.” Switzer explained, “we may be below that in the first quarter because first quarter is seasonally slower, particularly for mortgage and mortgage warehouse, but we'll be above that in the second half of the year, which would put us in that range for the full year.”
  • Zember detailed that “Primis Mortgage earned $1.4 million in the fourth quarter, which is about $1.8 million higher than '24,” and is modeling 2026 production in the “$1.6 billion to $2 billion range.”
  • Switzer provided guidance for expenses, stating, “our conservative estimate for our quarterly core expense range next year adjusted for mortgage and Panacea is $23 million to $24 million in 2026.”

Financial Results

  • Switzer reported, “average earning assets increased 13% annualized in the fourth quarter,” and “deposits were up 10% annualized in the quarter.”
  • Noninterest-bearing deposits ended the year at $554 million or 16% of total deposits versus $439 million or 14% at the end of 2024.
  • Switzer noted, “mortgage revenue was solid in Q4 at $10 million versus $8.9 million in Q3,” with retail mortgage production 84% higher year-over-year for the fourth quarter.
  • On expenses, Switzer explained, “core noninterest expense on a comparable basis was approximately $21 million, putting us only slightly higher than our run rate for the past year.”

Q&A

  • Nicholas Lorenzoni, Stephens Inc.: Asked about warehouse balances for 2026. Switzer answered, “we're anticipating mortgage warehouse to average $500 million across the year...it will be, call it, $200 million to $250 million higher than the fourth quarter because of the seasonality.” Zember added, “getting to $500 million is only going to improve that number.”
  • Lorenzoni asked about overall loan growth. Switzer replied, "the core bank is probably somewhere in the $100 million...call it, 5%, 6%, 7%." Panacea is modeled at about $150 million, with warehouse expected to grow by about $200 million more.
  • Lorenzoni inquired about expense sensitivity to mortgage banking. Switzer stated, "that $23 million to $24 million is excluding mortgage, right? Because the mortgage is going to be volatile and scale with the revenue side."
  • Lorenzoni asked about ROA targets. Switzer responded, “our bogey is still for the full year, a 1% ROA.”
  • Christopher Marinac, Janney Montgomery Scott: Queried about expense "noise" and margin contribution to ROA. Switzer replied, "the vast majority of it is behind us. We may have a little bit in the first quarter, but should not be anywhere near as significant as the fourth quarter." Zember stressed, "there's virtually no pressure anywhere in our company for OpEx growth."
  • Marinac asked about deposit growth sources. Zember said, "new customer acquisition is key...almost 6,000 new customers to the bank." He added, "balances 3 years after you acquired a customer are almost double what they were in the first year."
  • Marinac further questioned mortgage hires. Zember explained, “we're definitely going to have more hires,” but Switzer noted, “it will be more incremental than the two large teams we had last year.”
  • Marinac addressed special mention loans. Switzer and Zember assured, “we don't see substandard on these, and we definitely don't see big impairments or losses.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were focused on sustainability of margin growth, warehouse and loan expansion, and operating expense levels, posing questions with a neutral to slightly positive tone as management provided specific growth targets and addressed concerns.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, with repeated references to “momentum,” “opportunity,” and a “pathway to getting to 1% and it being sustainable.” Switzer’s and Zember’s responses emphasized discipline in expenses and optimism on asset growth and profitability.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed heightened confidence, with less focus on credit risk and more attention to growth and profitability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 remains focused on achieving a 1% ROA, with expanded clarity on expense and asset growth targets versus the prior quarter’s broader strategic commentary.
  • The margin improved from 3.18% in Q3 to 3.28% in Q4, while noninterest-bearing deposit mix increased from 14% to 16%.
  • Mortgage production and profitability showed marked improvement, with more specific volume and earnings projections.
  • Analyst questions shifted from concerns about credit exposure and deposit stickiness in Q3 to growth sustainability and operating leverage in Q4.
  • Management’s tone remained confident, with greater emphasis on execution and scalability of core businesses, compared to Q3’s focus on operational stabilization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged “noise” from restructuring and one-time items in Q4, but expects this to diminish in coming quarters.
  • Questions regarding special mention loans were addressed with reassurances about collateral strength and borrower liquidity, with no anticipated impairments.
  • Expense growth is being tightly controlled, with management stating “there's virtually no pressure anywhere in our company for OpEx growth.”

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that the company is entering 2026 with momentum, driven by margin expansion, disciplined expense management, and robust growth in core deposits and mortgage banking. The stated goal is a sustainable 1% ROA for the year, supported by further margin gains, stable credit quality, and scalable business lines, positioning Primis Financial for continued improvement in profitability and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Market Voices: Trump picks Warsh; Exxon, Chevron on Venezuela
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks.

  • President Trump has selected former Federal Reserve Gov. Kevin Warsh as his nominee to succeed Jerome Powell as Fed chair.

"I have known Kevin for a long period of time, and have no doubt that he will go down as one of the GREAT Fed Chairmen, maybe the best. On top of everything else, he is 'central casting,' and he will never let you down. Congratulations Kevin!" Trump wrote in a post on Truth Social. 

  • Republican Sen. Thom Tillis, who sits on the Senate Banking Committee, said he plans to block Warsh's nomination until the Trump administration ends its criminal investigation into the Fed and Fed Chair Jerome Powell.

“I am pleased with the nominee; I think that by most accounts he’s well regarded,” Tillis told Bloomberg TV. “But we still have to clear the current matter,” Tillis said, adding that the investigation was “frivolous prosecution.”

Powell, who has been harshly criticized by Trump in recent months, is slated to step down as Fed chair at the end of May.

“We have some time between now and May for that investigation to go away and for the confirmation process to open up,” Tillis said.

  • Exxon Mobil (XOM) CEO Darren Woods said the Venezuelan government needs to implement major reforms before his company could consider investing in the country's oil industry. 

“Those priorities start with one, stabilizing the country,” Woods told CNBC. “Second is to kickstart the economy and try to recover some of the damage that’s been done over the decades of abuse that the dictators brought in, and then ultimately to transition into representative government.”

  • Meanwhile, Chevron (CVX), which continued to operate in Venezuela during the Maduro regime, said the government has taken positive steps to help protect investments in its oil industry.

In an interview with CNBC, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said that contract security, commercial stability, and regulatory predictability were needed in order for the country to attract additional  investment.

“We see Venezuela taking steps in a positive direction to address those issues, which will encourage investment, not only from a company like ours but from others that I think are also considering the opportunities there,” Wirth told CNBC.

“We’ve stayed when others didn’t,” Wirth added. “We’re getting paid back some debts that we’re owed, and it does give us a significant head start. And the country has tremendous long-term potential.”

data.title: Moderna announces departure of medical chief
data.description:
Moderna (MRNA) announced on Friday that the company’s chief medical officer, Jacqueline Miller, will resign effective Mar. 2, a little over a year after taking over the role.

The Cambridge, Massachusetts-based company added that David Berman, who most recently served as R&D head at Immunocore (IMCR), will become the company's Chief Development Officer, effective the same date.

Miller previously served as the COVID-19 vaccine maker’s Senior Vice President, Therapeutic Area Head for Infectious Diseases, for more than four years before becoming the medical chief in November 2024.

She will also resign as a member of the company’s Executive Committee but will serve Moderna (MRNA) as a consultant to support the transition, the company said. Meanwhile, Berman will also become a member of the company’s Executive Committee and report to Chief Executive Officer Stéphane Bancel.

His appointment comes as Moderna (MRNA) pivots to newer mRNA-based medications such as personalized cancer therapies as demand for its COVID-19 vaccine declines.

“Over a two-decade career, Dr. Berman has worked on more than a dozen clinical-stage immunotherapies, including senior leadership roles in developing four oncology biologics,” Bancel said.

“I am deeply grateful for Jackie's leadership and significant contributions over the past five and a half years,” he added.

data.title: Chili's sizzle is eating fast food's lunch
data.description:
What started in the mid-1970s as a small Texas burger, taco, and chili spot has grown into Chili’s (EAT), a worldwide phenomenon known for its margaritas, baby back ribs, and its outsized contribution to its parent company’s earnings.

Founded by Larry Levine in 1975 in a converted post office outside Dallas, Texas, and named for the chili on its menu, Chili’s was bought in 1983 by Norman Brinker and became part of Brinker International’s (EAT) portfolio.

Today, there are more than 1,200 Chili’s restaurants in the U.S. with another 400 spread out across the globe—including the Middle East, Asia, and South America—making it the third most popular casual dining chain in the U.S. (after Texas Roadhouse and Olive Garden).

With the help of a generous advertising budget and a famous earworm jingle for its ribs, the company’s growth exploded, surpassing rival Applebee’s with $1M more sales per store and contributing the vast majority of Brinker International’s (EAT) $5.4B in annual revenue.

More specifically, the chain’s meteoric growth was driven by successful menu upgrades, restaurant upgrades, and effective social media marketing—including a viral “cheese pull" video—that lured in Gen Zers, Millennials, Baby Boomers, and everyone else in between.

And at a time when consumers grew increasingly frustrated by the high prices at fast food chains, Chili’s capitalized on its value proposition. With average checks approaching those at McDonald’s (MCD), diners reconsidered casual dining as a more affordable option.

As a result, Chili’s same-store sales grew an average of 12% between 2022 and 2025, outpacing the casual dining segment by as much as 680 basis points.

The turnaround can be credited to not just the menu, mozzarella sticks, and memorabilia, but also Kevin Hochman, who became Brinker’s (EAT) chief executive in 2022. Hochman went to work by focusing on three “”T's”—Traffic, Tickets, and Team. He reduced the number of menu items by 25%, reinvested in marketing (leaning into social media), and invested in staff and reduced the complexities in the kitchen (embracing the “Chilihead” culture).

“The Chili's turnaround is real, it is sustaining, and we have no intentions of taking our foot off the gas, which means we will continue to be focused on improving our food, service, and atmosphere, as well as continue making Chili's more fun, easier, and more rewarding for our team members,” Hochman said on the company’s most recent earnings call. 

And to sustain that growth, Chili’s is capitalizing on consumers’ shift from expensive beef to more affordable chicken with a “super premium chicken sandwich” lineup to be debuted in April with a “substantial” ad campaign.

“Chicken sandwiches is a very large market with over 80% of people buying them at least once last year and is by far the biggest segment of all restaurant chicken servings,” Hochman added. Even chains like McDonald’s (MCD) that were once heavily associated with burgers are now seeing a noticeable shift into chicken, selling equal amounts of burgers and chicken tenders.

This bodes well for Chili’s future growth, as the company has clearly shown its skill in luring in diners with inexpensive and tasty food and keeping the pressure on its fast-food rivals.

As consumers continue to trade down in search of value, Chili’s recent gains underscore how operational discipline and targeted marketing have helped the brand regain relevance, positioning it as a key driver of Brinker’s performance heading into the next fiscal year.

data.title: Wall Street's decline intensifies as tech trade sell-off deepens; Warsh picked as new Fed chair
data.description:
Wall Street's main averages were significantly lower on Friday as investors continued to assess the latest company earnings and technology stocks carried on declining.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.8% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.9%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1%.

All indexes are on track for weekly declines. Both the S&P 500 ((SP500)) and the Dow ((DJI)) are expecting monthly gains, but the Nasdaq ((COMP:IND)) might see losses on the month.

“Bulls have reason to smile: a positive January often sets the tone for a bullish year. When the S&P 500 (SP500) finishes the month higher, history shows full-year gains averaging 16.9% since 1950,” said Ryan Detrick, chief market strategist at Carson Group.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) prices continued to lower, now with the precious metals back around the $4,980/oz and $93/oz levels, respectively.

Investors turned their focus on U.S. President Donald Trump's selection of Kevin Warsh as the Federal Reserve chair.

“I have a tough time seeing Kevin Warsh being able to persuade his colleagues to a dovish position, a position that elevates employment risks over inflation and one he has never been able to make himself, even when the times demanded,” said Neil Dutta, head of economic research at Renaissance Macro.

“Markets tend to test new Fed chairs. The average correction in the first six months is 15%. When the chair faces their first crisis, investors don't wait and see how they handle it. Fed independence is a potential crisis for Warsh, but it could be anything,” said Ed Clissold, chief U.S. strategist for Ned Davis Research.

In other economic news, the producer price index came in hotter than expected in December. Also, the Chicago PMI rose far more than expected in January to 54.0, up from 42.7 in the prior month.

On earnings, Apple (AAPL) released its first quarter fiscal 2026 financial results that were driven by record-breaking iPhone sales. Shares were last -1.3%.

Also, Visa's (V) fiscal Q1 2026 earnings and revenue topped the average analyst estimates, but their stock was last -2.8%. KLA (KLAC) shares also dipped -12.6% even after its Q2 results topped estimates.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point higher at 4.25%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was 2 basis points lower at 3.55%.

data.title: The Bancorp targets $5.90 EPS for 2026 and $8.25 for 2027 with fintech initiatives and buybacks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Bancorp, Inc. (TBBK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Damian Kozlowski announced a new brand for The Bancorp, describing it as “a bold representation of the exciting future in front of us.” He reported, “The Bancorp earned $1.28 a share in the fourth quarter. EPS growth year-over-year was 11%.” Kozlowski highlighted that GDV increased 16% for the quarter versus the prior year, while credit sponsorship balances ended at $1.1 billion, up 40% from the third quarter and 142% year-over-year. He stated the company “exceeded our goal of at least $1 billion in credit sponsorship balances,” and is aiming to add at least two new partners in the coming year.
  • Kozlowski indicated that the embedded finance platform is on track for launch early in 2026 and that Cash App program implementation should meaningfully benefit GDV and fee revenue in 2026 and beyond. He added, “All 3 initiatives should be an increasingly positive effect on our financials as we move through '26 and show significant impact as we enter '27.”
  • Kozlowski reported criticized assets declined from $268 million to $194 million quarter-over-quarter and delinquency fell from 2.19% to 1.6% of loans.
  • CFO Dominic Canuso stated, “ROE was a record 30.4% in the quarter and 28.9% for the full year, continuing the trend of year-over-year improvements.” He detailed that ending assets increased to $9.4 billion, up 7% versus prior year and that “noninterest income, when excluding the credit enhancement, account for just over 30% of revenue in the quarter, with approximately 90% of fees coming from the fintech business.” Canuso also noted that net charge-offs and provision for loans both declined significantly quarter-over-quarter. He added, “Noninterest expense for the quarter was $56.2 million and included $2 million from a legal settlement relating to a previously disclosed legal proceeding.” The company repurchased $150 million of stock in the quarter, totaling $375 million for the year.

Outlook

  • Kozlowski announced, “We are initiating guidance of $5.90 EPS for 2026. We are targeting at least $1.75 a share in the fourth quarter 2026. We are maintaining a preliminary guidance for '27 of $8.25 a share.” He stated that 2026 buybacks are forecasted at $200 million total or $50 million a quarter. Kozlowski highlighted the expected impact of fintech initiatives, platform restructuring, AI tools, and continued buybacks as key EPS drivers, with gains subject to fintech development timelines and stock price for buybacks.

Financial Results

  • The Bancorp earned $1.28 per share in Q4 2025, with year-over-year EPS growth of 11%. GDV rose 16% for the quarter versus prior year. Revenue growth for the quarter, excluding credit enhancement income, was 3% year-over-year. For the full year, GDV grew 17% and total fee growth reached 21%. Ending assets increased to $9.4 billion, and the total loan portfolio increased $919 million to $7.26 billion, driven by $644 million in consumer fintech loans. Average deposits for the quarter were $7.6 billion with an average cost of 177 basis points. Provision for loans was $858,000, down from $5.8 million in the previous quarter, and net charge-offs were $629,000, down from $3.3 million. Noninterest expense was $56.2 million, including a $2 million legal settlement.

Q&A

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: Asked for detail on EPS ramp and key drivers. Kozlowski responded that “they're large revenue opportunities,” and that there is now “more clarity... than we did last -- end of last quarter where we did not issue preliminary guidance for 2026,” expressing confidence in meeting the $175 million target for the end of 2026.
  • Yanchunis: Asked about drivers weighing on Q4 results. Canuso cited legal fees, the government shutdown’s impact on payments and deposits, and timing of credit sponsorship growth, most of which occurred late in the quarter.
  • Yanchunis: Queried about off-balance sheet deposits. Canuso explained the strategy is to optimize earnings and monetize excess liquidity as deposit growth continues.
  • Emily Noelle Lee, KBW: Asked about REBL book refinancings and the Aubrey property. Canuso noted some refinancings involved new partners and lower yields, with properties in stronger positions. Regarding Aubrey, Canuso said the property is nearing cash flow breakeven and expects to seek an exit after stabilization, while Kozlowski emphasized a prudent exit for maximum value.
  • Lee: Asked about fintechs obtaining bank charters. Kozlowski stated, “We don't see an impact right now on our portfolio, and we don't expect to have a major impact in the future,” citing scalability and cost advantages.
  • Lee: Probed further into off-balance sheet deposit economics. Canuso indicated revenue generation will increase as deposit growth outpaces loan growth.
  • Unknown Analyst, Oppenheimer: Asked about $102 million in criticized REBL loans. Kozlowski described a diversified portfolio mostly in Southeast states, with no single market concentration.
  • Yanchunis: Sought outlook for credit sponsorship portfolio. Kozlowski projected it could “easily be double where we are today at the end of '26.” Canuso discussed expected NIM compression as the fintech mix grows, with profitability increasing as fee revenue rises.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally probing, focusing on the sustainability of EPS guidance, drivers of Q4 results, off-balance sheet deposit strategy, and risks in real estate and fintech portfolios. The tone was neutral, with clarifying follow-up questions and requests for transparency on key variables.
  • Management’s tone during prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing new guidance, fintech momentum, and credit quality improvements. In the Q&A, executives provided detailed explanations, expressing confidence in guidance and business model resilience, with Kozlowski stating, “We're very clear on the... program implementation guidelines, we now can better predict where we're going to be at the end of the year.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious (no 2026 guidance previously) to confident, with specific EPS targets and more clarity on fintech initiatives. Analysts’ tone was consistent, seeking specifics on execution and risk but not overtly skeptical.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company shifted from providing no specific 2026 guidance in Q3 to issuing detailed EPS targets for 2026 and 2027 in Q4. Strategic focus advanced from foundational fintech initiatives to execution and scaling, with a new brand launch and progress on embedded finance. Analysts shifted from seeking clarity on program launches (e.g., Cash App) and criticized asset resolution in Q3 to drilling into EPS trajectory, deposit strategy, and fintech portfolio growth in Q4. Management’s confidence increased, now offering precise targets and timelines. Key metrics such as credit sponsorship balances and criticized assets showed marked improvement from the previous quarter, and the company is now signaling material balance sheet optimization to support fintech growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged some Q4 headwinds, including unexpected legal fees and the government shutdown’s impact on payments and deposits. Timing of credit sponsorship growth was also cited as a factor, with most occurring at the end of the quarter. Executives expressed confidence that these factors are largely behind them and do not expect a repeat in Q1 2026. In real estate, management stressed prudent exit strategies for difficult assets and portfolio diversification to manage risk. No major threats were seen from fintechs seeking bank charters, given the company’s platform scalability and cost advantages. Management highlighted ongoing liquidity optimization and balance sheet flexibility to support fintech expansion.

Final Takeaway

The Bancorp, Inc. closed 2025 with strong earnings momentum, issuing new EPS targets of $5.90 for 2026 and $8.25 for 2027, supported by fintech initiatives, embedded finance launch, and continued stock buybacks. Management reported substantial progress in credit sponsorship and asset quality, while conveying confidence in meeting ambitious growth and profitability goals through focused execution and platform scalability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
